speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven, interview of Adam Boren, August 14th, 1996. Adam, who did your friend meet at this picnic?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picnic, the [INAUDIBLE] picnic, in Bad Nauheim-- he met a girl. And in conversation, she said she's from Warsaw, and he is from Warsaw. And she asked him what school did he go, and he mentioned the public school, to which I went. And she said, oh, I had a cousin that went there, to that school. And he asked, what was his name? And she says, Adam Borensztejn. He says Adam is in Hamburg. And unknown to me, when I came home that-- that evening, my aunt was sitting in my apartment in Hamburg. My-- that's-- my aunt is my father's sister. And they all survived. My aunt survive as a nurse in the German army with her tiny daughter, as a Ukrainian. She was serving as a nurse. My [INAUDIBLE] older daughter was in a Polish convent as a Christian. And my uncle was hidden in Polish family. So all of them survived. The whole family survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were registered to come to the United States. They got a visa. And when I told her that I am registered to come to Palestine, she says, where are you going to go alone? You're the only one in our family that is survived. Come with us. And I let myself be talked into. I'd-- if I went to Israel, by now I would have been a general or something of that sort. I sometimes resent it, but anyway, this is a wonderful country. So-- How did you get to the United States after this decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So we-- I moved to Bad Nauheim, where they lived, and went to Frankfurt and registered with the consulate for visa. There was a committee organized by United States Congress, act of Congress, called United States Committee for Care of European Orphan Children. And I pay my visa, and with that group, we were transported to Bremen-- Bremen Hafen, which is the exit port. Unfortunately, in that period, there was American sea-- sea ship strike or something for about a month. So we sat in the camp. And after the strike, we were shipped by liberty ships that Kaiser built that were troop carriers. They call them liberty ships. There were 300, 400 people in a barrack, in a multi deck things. Were brought to the yard. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to New York in-- I think it was in December '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your first impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first impression, we-- at night, the-- the ship docked right in front of the Statue of Liberty. It was at night, waiting for next morning. It just threw an anchor there. And we all were on the deck watching the traffic on the West Side Highway. And you can imagine that there was not traffic in Europe. There were no cars in Europe. And here, the lights and the buildings-- and there were no lights, and the traffic. And everybody on the boat was out all night long looking at the Liberty and-- and the traffic on the West Side. The next morning, we docked. We were brought-- picked up by the-- the children that were brought on that transport were of all denomination-- Jewish, Christian, Protestant, Catholics. And each of the welfare organization, like Catholic Welfare, Jewish HIAS, picked up its group. And we were sent to Hebrew National Orphan Home in Tuckahoe, New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was a wonderful home. They had everything. They had tennis courts and basketball courts and soccer courts and swimming pools and television, which was rare. I'm talking 1946. We have dancing where girls from high schools used to come in to our place. They were the best basketball team around. We had wonderful director, Dr. Kaftov. There was a family he introduced to me to that invite me for dinner, one of the Big Brothers who supported the house who wanted to adopt me, and-- because they lost a son in a war, a soldier. But I did-- was not adoptable. But I met some that were outright idiots, some of the Big Brothers. There was one that was bragging to me that had-- he was importing oil, bringing oil from Venezuela, and had the war lasted another year or so, he would have been multimillionaire. And another one, I had that-- in English, by the way, because we could speak English-- told me that wasn't so good in the States during the war. All he had is chicken, chicken, chicken. There was no beef at all available. And I felt very sorry for him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, how did you learn English in your early months?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learn English in two ways. One, I listened to radio broadcast. I went to movies and watch very closely. I tried to learn some of the-- some of the pronunciation of English words just in front of a mirror. But mostly, I learn by reading books that I knew well as a child. I read English or American authors-- Jack London and Lewis and whatever the authors that I used to swallow them. I read every Jack London book and Sinclair Lewis and Upton Sinclair and others, Western books-- the-- all the things as a child I went through [? series. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I read those same book in English, was easy for me to understand the sentence without going to dictionary, because many words were based on Latin, which I learned in school. And I could understand the meaning. And slowly, I started learning. Of course, I had problems. I could speak for 10 minutes and nobody knew if I spoke about a male or female, because I could say 'she, she, she,' and it was said boy I was talking about, you know? But I learned. I stayed in the orphan home for just a few months. And I left it. I said, I-- I don't want to stay. I want to-- I want to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody gave me-- one of the Big Brothers found for me a room in Bronx. Well, I moved in, and I got the first job in a garment industry. The same fellow found for me packaging together bundles of cut cloth. I became very good at it, put strings around it. And I did it for a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked the cutter, one of the cutters, could I learn cutting, because I don't intend to stay there and package goods, you know? So during-- when he finished his cutting, he gave me the knife, which is a rotary knife, and says, go cut this line. And as he did that, the whistle blew, and the foreman stopped the whole shop. I were not allowed to pick up the knife because I was not union. So I ask him, how do I become a union? And I couldn't speak English. My English was just beginning, but translated through Yiddish and German. I could become member if my father was a member, and he died. I could become member. I said, unfortunately, my father was not a member, and you cannot become-- so how do I learn? And he says, you can't. So the boss came in and he says, what did you do? I said, thank you very much. I'm quitting. I don't intend to package [INAUDIBLE], you know? And I became a electrician. Being that I work in Germany in Auschwitz in electrical, I became a house electrician, and very good one at that. How long did you work as an electrician?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I did it for a year or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was terrible, because I worked in Harlem, New York, converting from DC to AC. Many houses in Harlem, was their direct current. And it was horrible-- the cockroaches and wild cats and rats in a basement. You work in a basement, was not for my liking too much. I worked and I was good at it. And friend of mine was in television repairs. He was an engineer. And I switch off. I became a television mechanic, work for a company for about six months or so. When I was-- was hitchhiking and the fellow that I was hitchhiking told me that I shouldn't get stuck in New York. I should travel. Get out of New York. Go west. Go anywhere. And I at that time, Denver opened up a television station, just opened. And they were advertising, looking for mechanics, servicemen, for television. And so I went to Denver.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Denver?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay in Denver for about three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you leave Denver?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a girlfriend in New York, very bright girl. She was a valedictorian. I was in love with her. And we corresponded together. And she was very attractive, slim, had ponytails, which was-- means that I had no much-- not much hair. I always attracted to it. And I got one day 'Dear John' letter. And that's the first time I got into depression. I was shocked. I left everything. In fact, what happened-- I was working as a serviceman in Denver. And the company I was working for went bankrupt. It was a [INAUDIBLE] company. It was a New York company.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I and another fellow in the company bought out, at $0.10 on $1-- they gave it to us, you know, just to continue. And we ran the service in sales-- small, small, on a small way. But I left everything and I went to New York. And of course, she was ready to get married. So I was gone. So I stayed with my friends. And I wound up in New York. I lived with my friend when I got greetings from the president for induction in Korean War-- in Korean War, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what? In 1950 some of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Early '50s.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it. Yeah. And I went for examination. I passed at A1. And they gave you two weeks or three weeks to straighten out your business. So I went to New Jersey, where my cousin, that same cousin that found in [INAUDIBLE]. They lived in Asbury Park. Just to-- I quit my job. I gave up my room. And I'm going to to stay with them in Asbury Park.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of their friends was a major in US Army, psychiatrist. And his television went kaput, [? plat. ?] And I was telling him I was serviceman. He says, can you look at my television? So I went to his-- in Fort Mammoth, which is right here. He stayed there-- and fix his television.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are talking, and I said, I'm getting to the Army. And he knew in conversation I was concentration. And he says, they shouldn't take you, because they shouldn't-- they don't take former prisoners of war. If you are a prisoner of war, you're not inducted again. And you are all alone. You have no family. You shouldn't take [INAUDIBLE]. He says, I'll give you a letter. And some of my friends, by the way, went into the Army, fought in Korea-- survivors. I didn't know what the hell-- was a sealed letter. I went to Governor's Island. That's where the induction was. And that was the First Army. I give them the letter. They call everybody name, except me. I was sitting. They raised their hands, swore, and disappeared. And I sat there for hours. Finally, a guy came in, called me to a doctor's office. They had a room. The doctor read the letter. He tell me, tell me a little about what-- and I just gave him five minutes, what happened. He gave me a piece of [INAUDIBLE]. It was at [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I walk out. And the MP says, what the hell is wrong with you? You look pretty healthy to me. [LAUGHS] I was gone. I came back. And he said, hey, what did you do? Desert already? [LAUGHS] [? My ?] friends, they gave me a party, a go away party. So I stayed in Asbury Park. And I started a-- I learn in Colorado a business in evaporative coolers for cooling, very inexpensive way. So I started that in-- in New Jersey in Asbury Park with the husband of my cousin, of [? Lucia. ?] And we succeeded. We are reasonably successful. I mean, it's very small. But just, we didn't take-- I lived on like $20 a week, you know. And he-- he had worked for his father and were only part time in the business. So we kept growing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a friend of mine in New York with whom I stayed before, called me one day and says, I met a really interesting girl at a party. And I want you to meet her. And I said, Johnny, get off my back. I have all the girls I need. I have plenty. And I-- I still had hair. And-- don't make sense now. But you must meet her. I promised-- I told her about you, and she wants to meet you. And he bugging me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I called her and find out she's in the hospital. She has tore her [INAUDIBLE]. So I gave it up, and he called me back. She's back home. Oh, fine, I says, OK. I took her out. What year would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months later, we are married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name. Her maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name? Goldbarten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-L-D-B-A-R-T-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date of your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 10th, 1961.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you have two lovely children. Will you please tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had a, a couple of years after we were married-- I don't know why she married me. But anyway, we had a daughter, Sari-- she wonderful child. And of course, she grew up. She's-- she graduated from local high school, and then went to Brandeis University in Boston. And then he got-- she master from Harvard in special education. She now works for the Boston Museum of-- Computer Museum as special project, teaching project, for teachers and computers. It's a unique computer-- it's the only one in the world, which a teaching museum-- it's not a show museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And your other child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other son, Jonathan, we adopted. He was only a couple of weeks old when we adopted him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in 1971.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the good looking kid that you see here. And he was wonderful. In fact, when he was a boy, I let him do anything. Just like my mother let me do everything, I wanted him to be a tough kid no matter what. I didn't care if he fell off the roof. He was barefoot running, [INAUDIBLE] on a tree, on the roof. I give him too much freedom, I think, and not enough control. But in any case, he had artistic inclination. He painted very young. He went to San Francisco School of Fine Arts. He graduated from it. He lives now in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. And now, I know that you have recently retired. What was the business that you retired from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- first, we were in the-- in the evaporative cooler business. Then, the company that manufactured evaporative coolers was also in commercial refrigeration, making refrigerated display cases that used in supermarkets. And they asked us if we were-- because we were so successful with evaporative coolers-- if we wanted to distribute-- in the New Jersey area, they had no representative. And we took that on and became very successful. We put several supermarkets, completely from A to Z, in New Jersey area and refrigeration and air conditioning, commercial. One of our customer was a bakery. The owner of the bakery was a young man, my age, who became friend of mine. His name is Herbert Friedman. It's Friedman Bakeries. He went for a trip to Holland, to Europe, first time. He met-- he was always, when he was on a trip-- and I went on several trips with him-- he always went looking, visiting bakeries. In one bakery in Holland, Amsterdam, he saw a machine that could make bread-- special bread that they were having here that was handmade-- rye bread. It would revolutionize his working. So he found out where this machine is made. It was made in the La Hague then The Hague, Holland. He met the manufacturer, told him the problems, and the manufacturer assured them that the machine would do it. If not, he would make it do it. He purchased the machine, shipped it to the United States. The machine did not work. So he called the manufacturer, and the manufacturer came in, made-- he was brilliant, the Dutch manufacturer. He made the machine, converted it, and it did perform. And he did the repairs in our machine shop. We had-- in our refrigeration business, we had a machine shop, so the conversion. Then, by word of mouth, this flour salesman told other bakers about the molder former that forms the bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept calling and saying, hey, we'd like to buy it one. Where do we get it? So Herbie and I, being good friends, I say, why don't we start a company? And we import and we have fun. We're both single. We travel to Europe. And you got your bakeries, we have our refrigeration. And we started that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a rather short time with him-- two, three years-- we started manufacturing a different design machinery for all production, not for bread. Became the only and biggest manufacturer of bake-- automatic bakery machinery and ovens in the United States for European-type breads, not for white breads or hot dogs or hamburgers. It was called Adamatic. That was a ego complex of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you sell that business?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1989, a company Hobart, who was international food producing, machinery producer, came to us, started romancing us. They wanted to buy us. And after several months of negotiation, made us an offer we couldn't refuse, and because my son was not interested and nobody else, and we were all getting-- in the '60s, we sold it. They paid for this house, by the way-- Hobart. That was-- Hobart's house, I call it. I was two years ago on a medical examination which had nothing to do with cancer, where they put a tube in your mouth. I don't know what they call it, this thing-- a doctor in New York. He came out-- I was still under narcosis-- and told Claire, who was all alone, I have cancer of pancrea in advanced stage and maybe six months to live. He recommended us to top doctors in New York, a oncologist was a professor of Mount Sinai. The surgeon, Dr. [? Cooperman, ?] is world renowned, specializing in the surgery of-- of-- of pancrea. They have a name for it, that type of surgery. I forgot what it is. I went through a very intensive, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very intensive radiotherapy-- what they call it-- radiation therapy and chemotherapy, where the tumors was shrunk. They wouldn't operate in the beginning. They say it's non-operative-- shrunk sufficiently that the surgeon operated. Was very difficult because, these other organs were encompassed. He said, normally, it takes him two hours, was over four and a half hours. He had to scrape it just millimeter by millimeter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I recovered. And there is no further sign-- Isn't that-- that is wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so far, and it's two years. So [INAUDIBLE], but if it didn't, didn't bother me. When they told me six months, I says, OK, I got-- I should have been dead 50 or ago. C'est la vie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you want to add to the tape about your experiences in the Holocaust? Was there an episode in Majdanek that--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was an incident in Majdanek where I was working near a pool, cleaning-- cleaning food kettles, where a SS man kicked me into the pool, which was very deep. It was a fire pool, water in case of fire. And each time I tried to grab the rim, he would step on my hands. And I would have drowned, because I had no strength. I was close-- where they called him, and he walked away, and they pulled me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, I was-- I was-- that was my [? sister ?] [INAUDIBLE]. So it's nothing to be worry about. In fact, my saying is that-- it's nothing new, but that we are all born with a terminal disease. We're gonna die. It's only a matter when-- not that we have to live with that thought. We live to-- to enjoy life, to help others. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it is a fact, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore. I used to. I used to have nightmares. I used to wake up in the middle of the night, screaming, sometimes beating Claire-- in the middle, but not anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, experience what we're heading to now will give you a couple sleepless nights, but not-- nothing terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you tell your children about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I talked to my children almost from the time that were were capable of understanding. We are very, very active. Now, when I-- when I moved to Denver, I was disgusted of being a Jew, I think. It-- it's-- it's a terrible, terrible experience. Why should I continue it? It's most idiotic thing, ridiculous. The-- the history of it, although it has many, many glorious time, had so much persecution.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I-- I'll give it up. The hell with it. And then, it was very easy. There were no Jews in Denver. You know? I don't know any while I was there. But then I came to conclusion that it will be a sin, that, you know, would be another victory for Hitler. And I turned around, became very active, Claire and I, in the Jewish community, we were. Claire was a president of the women division. I was a vice president, an honorary president of the-- of the whole Mammoth County, in many positions. And of course, in the [INAUDIBLE], I was. And in fact, my children used to call me the super Jew. You know? They never saw us. Every evening, we were somewhere other, some other things. But I told them very early. Ok.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, they don't wanna-- my daughter, who-- Sari, while she was at Brandeis, she worked as a volunteer in video production with a professor who was a video professor. And he-- she was a assistant producer. So she brought once a camera similar to that camera, and she took my statements on her being the operator, probably, not near as good as she. But-- and she never wanted to show me the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they know everything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What message would you like to give the world about your experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing that I try to-- I don't know what success. When I speak, I speak to the high schools. I speak to general schools. I speak yearly to US army in Fort Mammoth-- is that simple, supposedly innocent, expression demeaning any group, being the Jews or the Italians or the Greeks or the [? Huns, ?] dehumanizing will lead to murder. It is the first step, because if you dehumanize a person, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can then as easily step on him as you step on a bug. A-- Vietnam, they call them-- I forgot, there was a demeaning name-- gooks, [? kings, ?] whatever. Well, then you can shoot them-- you can be against any people. To be aware of it-- unfortunately, people don't do it. But that's the greatest danger, as Jews, we have experienced through centuries. And you would think that the world has learned what happens. But it doesn't. Unfortunately, it doesn't. Not only that, the anti-Semitism continues. There were no Jews in Poland. There's less than 5,000 Jews in Poland. But I was just there a year and a half, and there slogans, [? people ?] [? around ?] the world, the most anti-Semitic, horrible slogans, just like there were during Nazi time. Same thing happens in former Yugoslavia-- you know, one religion on another. The thing in Ireland, the hatred-- [INAUDIBLE] there I once found a brochure on a plane flying from London to Leeds-- the same plane goes to Ireland-- that was taken word for word from Streicher anti-Semitic magazine, showing the pope with a big nose dripping. That was the Protestant thinks demeaning the-- I mean, horror.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says, I hope that the world one day learned-- if they religious, that we are all children of God, and if they not, that life is sacred thing. And there are not better human beings or more holy than others. There are good and bad in all of us. Fortunately, there are some good people. There are the bad one, but there are good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I just want to say that it's been an honor to interview you, Adam. Thank you very much for taking part in this project.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is a photograph, a photo montage of a family from my father's side, part of the family. On the bottom, starting from left to right, is my uncle, youngest uncle, Fredek. Next is a little boy named Adam, who is the son of the lady holding him, my aunt, and my uncle, my father's brother, Sojnek. Next is a couple, my aunt, my father's sister, Adela, and her husband, who immigrated to United States long before the war, and passed away. The top row, starting from left to right, is Rena, my father's sister. Next to her is her sister, my aunt, Tofila, with her son, also called Adam. Next, the couple to the right are my grandfather-- his name was Godel Borensztejn-- and his wife, my grandmother, Pesa.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture it was a composite taken by my cousin, who passed away in United States, from individual photographs.
